content.assessment: As an explicit no-policy counterfactual, this proposal is not a reform bundle at all, which is directly relevant to the lens's causal-attribution question: any observed contraction or performance change under this baseline cannot be attributed to policy design, since it is driven purely by demographic mechanics. The lens is well-suited to flag that this baseline, while methodologically useful for isolating the causal contribution of active interventions (T1-T4, T6), does not itself constitute or substitute for whole-system reform, and its own risk section concedes Roma segregation and church-sector selective growth are unaddressed.
opportunities: Serves as the necessary comparison baseline against which the causal contribution of T1-T4/T6 can be isolated, strengthening the evidentiary basis for evaluating those proposals
strengths: Provides a methodologically necessary counterfactual for causal attribution when evaluating other proposals, directly serving the lens's own causal-attribution criterion
Demographic mechanisms (cohort decline, class-size thresholds) are well-documented as durable structural trends operating independently of policy choice, making the baseline empirically grounded
threats: Uncoordinated closures could trigger settlement-viability disruption without any phased management, a risk structurally similar to (if smaller than) the Polish transition-cost pattern

Absence of active policy means none of the documented segregation mechanisms (tracking, church growth, Roma exclusion) are addressed, so this baseline cannot be mistaken for a reform achieving the stated equity goal
weaknesses: Passive contraction is geographically uneven and could worsen geographic equity even as aggregate selective-track size shrinks, contradicting any implicit assumption that shrinkage alone improves equity
Roma segregation and church-sector selective growth are not addressed at all, meaning the equity gaps most clearly documented in the evidence base persist or could worsen under this baseline
